titleStmt.title: Schrieffer-Wolff transformation for non-Hermitian systems: application for PT -symmetric circuit QED

analytic.title: Schrieffer-Wolff transformation for non-Hermitian systems: application for PT -symmetric circuit QED

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: Combining non-hermiticity and interactions yields novel effects in open quantum many-body systems. Here, we develop the generalized Schrieffer-Wolff transformation and derive the effective Hamiltonian suitable for various quasi-degenerate non-Hermitian systems. We apply our results to an exemplary PT -symmetric circuit QED composed of two non-Hermitian qubits embedded in a lossless resonator. We consider a resonant quantum circuit as |ωr -Ω| ≪ ωr, where Ω and ωr are qubits and resonator frequencies, respectively, providing well-defined groups of quasi-degenerate resonant states. For such a system, using direct numerical diagonalization we obtain the dependence of the low-lying eigenspectrum on the interaction strength between a single qubit and the resonator, g, and the gain (loss) parameter γ, and compare that with the eigenvalues obtained analytically using the effective Hamiltonian of resonant states. We identify PT -symmetry broken and unbroken phases, trace the formation of Exceptional Points of the second and the third order, and provide a complete phase diagram g -γ of low-lying resonant states. We relate the formation of Exceptional Points to the additional P-pseudo-Hermitian symmetry of the system and show that non-hermiticity mixes the "dark" and the "bright" states, which has a direct experimental consequence.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Seminal Schrieffer-Wolff transformation [1][2][3](also known as Van Vleck Quasi-Degenerate Perturbation theory [4][5][6][7][8] in some other contexts) has been used for many years as an extremely useful tool in a theoretical study of weakly interacting quantum systems. The Schrieffer-Wolff transformation allows one to consistently derive the effective low-energy Hamiltonian composed of the noninteracting part and the interaction terms taken into account as a perturbation. Generally, Schrieffer-Wolf transformation was successfully applied to the perturbation analysis of various quantum-mechanical problems arising in electronic or optical systems, such as the Kondo problem in the Anderson impurity model in normal metals or superconductors [2,9], multiorbital Hubbard models [10], Floquet spectrum of periodically driven systems [11], interacting qubits [12][13][14][15][16][17][18]. Moreover, the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation was also adapted to intrinsically dissipative quantum systems described by Markovian master equations [19] and, more recently, discussed in the context of some non-hermitian systems [20,21].

A particular example of the qubits-type systems coined as circuit QED, i.e., various arrays of superconducting qubits coupled to a low dissipative resonator, presents a special interest for quantum computing, quantum simulations, and precise quantum measurements [12,13,18,22]. Even a weak coupling of qubits to the resonator leads to numerous interesting phenomena, e.g., AC Stark shift of qubits frequencies [12,23], or the interaction between well separated qubits mediated by the exchange of virtual photons of the resonator [12,24,25], and the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation are routinely used to analyze these * Grigorii.Starkov@rub.de effects.

Recently, this field of study has got a new twist as so-called PT -symmetric non-Hermitian qubits systems have been experimentally realized in various solid state systems such as trapped ions, ultracold and Rydberg atoms [26][27][28], Bose-Einstein condensate [29], superconducting [30][31][32] or nitrogen-vacancies qubits [33]. In a PT -symmetric non-Hermitian qubit, specially chosen quantum states demonstrate a nonequilibrium growth of the population, i.e., the states with a gain. To establish the PT -symmetry the gain has to be completely equalized by a loss present in other parts of a system.

A general theoretical study of PT -symmetric non-Hermitian Hamiltonians has been started by the works of Bender and coworkers [34][35][36]. They have shown that depending on the values of physical parameters the PT -symmetric non-Hermitian Hamiltonian exhibits two kinds of energy spectrum: purely real eigenvalues, identifying the unbroken (preserved) PT -symmetric quantum phase, or complex conjugate ones, indicating the appearance of broken PT -symmetry quantum phase. The Exceptional Points (EP s) separate these phases.

Systems of PT -symmetric interacting qubits (twolevels or spin 1/2) have been theoretically studied for two PT -non-Hermitian qubits with an exchange interaction [37] as well as in the framework of the quantum Ising model with a longitudinal [38,39] or transverse non-Hermitian fields [40]. At the same time, one should stress that in most previous works a theoretical study of the behavior of eigenvalues and eigenvectors near an n-th order EP has been carried out using the local effective n × n Hamiltonian. Therefore, a study of the relation between the EP s arising in the presence of interaction between qubits, and the properties of non-interacting qubits system did not receive a proper attention so far.

In this Article, we develop the generalized Schrieffer-Wolff transformation for arbitrary non-Hermitian inter-acting systems and derive the effective Hamiltonian in which the interaction is treated as the perturbation up to the second order. Since the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation is especially suited for quantum systems with well-defined groups of quasi-degenerate states [3], we apply our generic analysis to an exemplary PT -symmetric quantum system, i.e., a PT -symmetric circuit QED composed of two non-Hermitian qubits weakly coupled to a lossless resonator. The non-Hermiticity is introduced in the circuit QED model in the form of an imaginary longitudinal magnetic field of a strength, γ, or equivalently as a gain (loss) of corresponding energy levels of a single qubit. The resonant regime will be addressed as the qubit's frequency Ω is assumed to be close to the frequency of a resonator, ω r [12,18]. Combining the direct numerical diagonalization with the analysis based on the derived effective Hamiltonian, we study the lowenergy spectrum of PT -symmetric circuit QED in detail. We identify various PT -symmetry preserved and broken quantum phases, and quantitatively analyze the formation of EP s of different orders.

The outline of the paper is as follows. In Section II we formulate the general Schrieffer-Wolff transformation suitable for quasi-degenerate non-Hermitian Hamiltonian systems. Using such transformation, we derive the effective Hamiltonian. In Section III we introduce a basic model of PT -symmetric circuit QED, i.e., two non-Hermitian qubits embedded in a lossless resonator, discuss important symmetries of a system, and, using the direct numerical diagonalization, obtain the dependence of the eigenspectrum on the coupling strength, g, and the gain (loss) parameter, γ. In Section IV, we use the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to derive the effective Hamiltonian matrix of the resonant states and discuss its symmetry properties. After that, in Section V, using the effective Hamiltonian we study the formation of Exceptional Points in detail, and produce a complete phase diagram of the low-lying resonant states. In Section VI the conclusions will be provided. There are also Appendix A, where we compute the matrix elements of the effective Hamiltonian, and Appendices B, C and D, where the derivation details regarding the phase diagram and EP s are presented.

div.head: II. SCHRIEFFER-WOLFF TRANSFORMATION FOR A QUASI-DEGENERATE NON-HERMITIAN HAMILTONIAN
div.p: Let us consider a quantum system described by an arbitrary non-Hermitian Hamiltonian of the form

where both Ĥ0 and V can be non-Hermitian. We assume that the main part of the Hamiltonian, Ĥ0 , has a well-defined group of quasi-degenerate eigenstates which is clearly separated from other levels in energy. The pa-rameter g is considered to be small, and we will treat the second term g V as a perturbation. We further assume, that Ĥ0 is not close to the EP s and it has a complete biorthonormal basis of right |R i ⟩ and left ⟨L i | eigenvectors [41]:
These eigenvectors satisfy the condition [42]

Let P be the set of quasi-degenerate eigenstates of the Hamiltonian Ĥ0 that we want to project onto, and let Q = P be the set of all other states. In the non-Hermitian case, the projector operator P on the set of states P is defined as [19,43]:
We also introduce the orthogonal projector:
written explicitly as

Using the biorthogonality of the left and right eigenvectors, one can check that the projector operators P and Q satisfy the following properties: P 2 = P , Q2 = Q and P Q = Q P = 0. The use of the projector operators, P and Q, allows one to split the Hamiltonian as [7,8]:
div.formula: Ĥ = Ĥ0 + g P V P + Q V Q VD +g P V Q + Q V P VX . (8)
div.p: Note, the projector operators P and Q commute with Ĥ0 . As a consequence, P Ĥ0 Q = Q Ĥ0 P = 0, and
div.formula: Ĥ0 = P Ĥ0 P + Q Ĥ0 Q (9)
div.p: The operator Ĥ0 + g VD yields the "diagonal" part of the Hamiltonian that does not mix the states from subspaces P and Q. On the contrary, the operator g VX gives rise to the "off-diagonal" part of the Hamiltonian connecting the states from different subspaces. Next, we search for the transformation allowing to get rid of the off-diagonal part g VX of the Hamiltonian (8). More precisely, one have to find the operator Ŝ, such that
div.formula: e g Ŝ Ĥ0 + g V e -g Ŝ = Ĥ′ ,(10)
div.p: where Ĥ′ satisfies
div.formula: P Ĥ′ Q = Q Ĥ′ P = 0. (11
div.p: The procedure described by Eqs. ( 10) and ( 11), defines the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation. For Hermitian Hamiltonian Ĥ, the operator Ŝ is an anti-Hermitian one, and Eq. ( 10) describes a unitary transformation. For a non-Hermitian case, the operator Ŝ acquires a Hermitian component, and Eq. ( 10) determines then a similarity transformation.
As usual, Eq. ( 10) is written as the series of chained commutators:
div.formula: e g Ŝ Ĥe -g Ŝ = Ĥ + +∞ n=1 g n n! Ŝ, Ŝ, . . . Ŝ n times , Ĥ .(12)
div.p: Expanding the operator Ŝ in a series of powers of g as

and substituting it in Eq. ( 12) we express the transformed Hamiltonian Ĥ′ also as a series in powers of g. Substituting the Hamiltonian (8) into Eq. ( 12) and keeping only the terms up to the second order in g, we find the effective Hamiltonian Ĥeff in the following form
div.formula: Ĥeff = Ĥ0 + g VD + VX + Ŝ0 , Ĥ0 + + g 2 Ŝ1 , Ĥ0 + Ŝ0 , VD + + Ŝ0 , VX + 1 2 Ŝ0 , Ŝ0 , Ĥ0 .(14)
div.p: To eliminate VX in the first order, we require
and Ŝ0 is obtained explicitly as
div.formula: Ŝ0 = P Û1 Q + Q Û2 P ,(16)
div.p: where
div.formula: Û1 = - p∈P,q∈Q |R p ⟩⟨L p | V |R q ⟩⟨L q | E 0 q -E 0 p , (17
) Û2 = q∈Q,p∈P |R q ⟩⟨L q | V |R p ⟩⟨L p | E 0 q -E 0 p . (18
div.p: Choosing the operator Ŝ1 to satisfy the condition [ Ŝ1 , Ĥ0 ] + [ Ŝ0 , VD ] = 0 we obtain the effective Hamiltonian as
div.formula: Ĥeff = Ĥ0 + g VD + g 2 2 Ŝ0 , Vx .(19)
div.p: Substituting Eq. ( 16) into Eq. ( 19), we find
div.formula: Ŝ0 , VX = P Û1 Q + Q Û2 P , P V Q + Q V P = = P Û1 Q V -V Q Û2 P + + Q Û2 P V -V P Û1 Q. (20
div.p: Since the effective Hamiltonian does not mix the two subspaces P and Q, one concludes that the second term on the r.h.s. of Eq. ( 20) does not contribute to the projected effective Hamiltonian P Ĥeff P . Taking that into account we finally obtain the explicit matrix elements of the effective Hamiltonian of a well-defined group of quasi-degenerate states, P ,
div.formula: ⟨L p | Ĥeff |R p ′ ⟩ = E (0) p δ p,p ′ + g⟨L p | V |R p ′ ⟩- - g 2 2 q∈Q 1 E 0 q -E 0 p + 1 E 0 q -E 0 p ′ × × ⟨L p | V |R q ⟩⟨L q | V |R p ′ ⟩.(21)
div.p: Note, the outlined procedure is quite general and can be extended to obtain the higher-order corrections in g.

div.head: III. TWO PT -SYMMETRIC QUBITS EMBEDDED IN A LOSSLESS RESONATOR: MODEL AND ENERGY SPECTRUM
div.p: To demonstrate the effectiveness of the non-hermitian version of the Schrieffer-Wolf transformation we consider a basic PT -symmetric circuit QED composed of two non-Hermitian qubits coupled to a lossless resonator. The Hamiltonian of such a system reads as
where the Hamiltonian of two non-interacting biased qubits is
div.formula: Ĥqb = 2 n=1 ∆ 2 σx n + ϵ 2 σz n + (-1) n iγ σz n .(23)
div.p: Here, σ(x,z) are the corresponding Pauli matrices, and ∆, ϵ (a qubit's bias) are real off-diagonal and diagonal matrix elements, accordingly. The non-hermiticity is introduced as a staggered imaginary longitudinal magnetic field or a gain/loss parameter of individual qubits, γ. All parameters of individual qubits are assumed to be identical.
The Hamiltonian Ĥres describes a single bosonic mode of the resonator with the frequency ω r
where ω r is the characteristic frequency of the resonator. The interaction between the qubits and the resonator is determined by the Hamiltonian Ĥint as

Identical coupling strengths, g = g 1 = g 2 , are assumed for simplicity. The parity P and the time-reversal T operators are defined for the system as the exchange of qubits and the complex conjugation operators, respectively [37,38,44]:
div.formula: P σr j P-1 = σr 3-j , T i T -1 = -i.(26)
div.p: Notice here that in the presence of the opposite signs of the gain/loss parameter for different qubits, i.e., a staggered gain/loss, the PT symmetry of the Hamiltonian ( 22) is preserved even in the biased regime, ϵ ̸ = 0. In addition to the PT symmetry, the Hamiltonian ( 22) is also P-pseudo-Hermitian, i.e.,

which will prove to be crucial for the qualitative understanding of the results. Next, by making use of the direct numerical diagonalization we compute the low-lying eigenspectrum of the Hamiltonian (22). The procedure described in detail in [37][38][39] is as follows: fixing the parameters ∆ and ϵ we vary the gain (loss) γ and the coupling strength, g. The maximum number of boson states used in the numerical analysis was n = 7. Such numerically obtained eigenspectrum was then used to identify the PT -symmetry preserved and broken quantum phases, and the EP s separating these phases.

Since the formation of EP s in the model is based on the parity-indices of the states due to its P-pseudo-Hermitian symmetry [38,39], we assign to each level a topological index equal to the parity of the level at γ = 0 [45]. This index is conserved in the region of parameters where an eigenvalue of the state stays real, and the second-order EP s are provided only by pairs of states with opposite parity-indices [39].
A. Hermitian circuit QED, γ = 0: longitudinal and transverse couplings

First, we recall the quantum-mechanical behavior of a basic circuit QED system in the absence of non-Hermitian terms, i.e., as γ = 0 [12,13]. In this case one can define the frequency Ω and the mixing angle θ of a single qubit as
div.formula: Ω = ∆ 2 + ϵ 2 , θ = arccos ϵ √ ∆ 2 + ϵ 2 . (28
div.p: Transforming the single-qubit Hamiltonian Ĥ1qb = (∆/2)σ x + (ϵ/2)σ z to the diagonal form we obtain two eigenvectors |±⟩ with corresponding eigenvalues ±Ω/2.
In a new representation the interaction Hamiltonian takes the form:
div.formula: Ĥint = g[cos θ(σ z 1 + σz 2 ) + sin θ(σ x 1 + σx 2 )](â † + â). (29
div.p: We choose the parameters to be close to the resonance condition, |ω r -Ω| ≪ Ω, precisely ω r /Ω = 1.07. The numerically obtained nine low-lying normalized energy levels, E/Ω, of Eq. ( 22) (for γ = 0) are presented in Figs. 1(a,b) as a function of normalized interaction strength g/Ω for two extreme interacting regimes: almost longitudinal (sin θ ≃ 0) and transverse (cos θ ≃ 0) couplings, respectively. The energy levels of a Hermitian circuit QED biased in the resonant regime demonstrate the groups of wellseparated quasi-degenerate states for g ≪ 1, and therefore, the Schrieffer-Wolf transformation are suitable for the analysis of such a system. We also relate the energy levels plotted in Fig. 1 to the eigenvectors at g = 0. In the order of the increasing energy the ground state corresponds to
the middle group of three states corresponds to
and the upper group of four states is
Here, the notation implies
where n is the number of bosons in the resonator. The parities of all these states are positive (+1) except of the states

which have negative (-1) parity. According to the Wigner-von Neumann selection rule [46], the latter states are decoupled from the rest of the states. For this reason, these negative parity states are called "dark" while the rest of the states are referred to as "bright". However, at non-zero γ the non-Hermitian terms in the P-pseudo-Hermitian Hamiltonian mix the "dark" and the "bright" states resulting in the second-order EPs [39].
div.formula: B. Eigenspectrum of PT -symmetric circuit QED, γ ̸ = 0: longitudinal coupling
div.p: To study the eigenspectrum of PT -symmetric circuit QED with an almost longitudinal coupling (θ = π/40 ≪ 1) between qubits and the resonator it is convenient to focus separately on the two groups of quasi-degenerate states that we identified in Fig. 1(a). In Figs. 2 and3, we display the real parts of the normalized eigenenergies, Re [E/Ω], as a function of the normalized interaction strength, g/Ω, for different fixed values of γ/Ω. The positive (negative) parities of the states are indicated by solid (dashed) lines. In Fig. 2, we focus on the group of three middle levels of Fig. 1(a), while in Fig. 3 we focus on the group of four upper levels. In the parameter regions where we have pairs of complex conjugated eigenvalues, we display the imaginary parts Im [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies as the shaded ribbons with the widths proportional to Im [E/Ω]. A pair of complex conjugated eigenvalues can appear, when two real-valued levels get together and pass through a second-order EP. Therefore, we identify the second-order EP s in Figs. 2 and3 as the points where the ribbon width shrinks to zero. As expected [39], the second-order EP s are formed between the states with opposite parity indices.

The panels (b, c) of Fig. 2 demonstrate the precursor of a third-order EP. One finds two second-order EP s separated by the region of parameters where the three levels have real eigenenergies. Moreover, these two secondorder EP s share only one level with opposite parity index. As γ/Ω increases, the intermediate region with three real eigenvalues shrinks until two EP s coalesce into one point, which is the third-order EP. For a resonant PT -symmetric circuit QED with an almost longitudinal coupling a third-order EP occurs for an extremely small value of γ/Ω ≃ 0.008.

The three upper levels of Fig. 3 also form a third-order EP. We show its precursor in panel (c) of Fig. 3. To conclude this subsection we notice that the higher energy levels (not shown in Fig. 1) also form well-separated groups of four quasi-degenerate levels that behave similarly to the one considered in Fig. 3.
div.formula: C. Eigenspectrum of PT -symmetric circuit QED, γ ̸ = 0: transverse coupling
div.p: In Fig. 4, we present the eigenspectrum of PTsymmetric circuit QED biased in the resonant regime in the presence of an almost transverse coupling for the qubit mixing angle, θ = π/2 -π/40, and different values of γ/Ω. Similarly to the previous subsection we focus on the groups of excited levels as in Figs. 2 and3, but display them together in this case.

Qualitative picture roughly does not change: the nonhermiticity leads to the formation of the second-order EP s between the levels with opposite parity indices. We also observe pairs of second-order EP s with shared level of opposite parity index that approach each other as γ increases. These pairs eventually coalesce into third-order EP s.

Nevertheless, there is also a significant difference with the case of almost longitudinal coupling: as the qubitresonator interaction g increases, the level repulsion between the states of the same parity index inside a resonant group is so strong, that it pushes the states from different groups towards each other and they go through avoided crossings. Therefore, the applicability of the Schrieffer-Wolf transformation for the PT -symmetric circuit QED with a transverse coupling is limited to the regime g/Ω ≪ 1. Here, we obtain analytically the eigenspectrum of PTsymmetric circuit QED by making use of the generic Schrieffer-Wolf transformation elaborated in Sec. II. We identify the main part Ĥ0 of the Hamiltonian (1) as Ĥ0 = Ĥqb + Ĥres , and the perturbation g V = Ĥint . We focus on a PT -symmetric circuit QED with a longitudinal coupling and on the quasi-degenerate group of states presented in Fig. 2.
A. Biorthonormal basis of the right and the left eigenvectors at g = 0.

Let us first discuss the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of unperturbed PT -symmetric circuit QED, i.e., as g = 0. The eigenvalues of the single-qubit Hamiltonian Ĥ1qb = (∆/2) σx 1 + (ϵ/2 + iγ) σz 1 are readily obtained as
The corresponding right eigenvectors are chosen as
As Ĥ1qb is described by a symmetric matrix, the corresponding left eigenvectors are obtained by the transposition:
The chosen right and left eigenvectors produce the biorthonormal basis states of a single qubit. Indeed, one can check that ⟨+ l |+ r ⟩ = ⟨l |r ⟩ = 1, and

The Hamiltonian of the second qubit Ĥ2qb is obtained from Ĥ1qb by complex conjugation. As the consequence, the eigenvalues and both right and left eigenvectors can be obtained by the complex conjugation of the expressions (34)- (38). At g = 0 the vectors
constitute the basis of the right eigenvectors of the Hamiltonian Ĥ0 with eigenenergies

Here, ∼ in | ±r ⟩ serves as a reminder that the eigenenergies and the eigenvectors of the second qubit are obtained by complex conjugation of ( 34)- (38). The corresponding basis of the left eigenvectors is

Notice that the normalized boson states |n⟩ are simultaneously the right and the left eigenvectors, so they do not have any r/l subscripts. Thus defined eigenvectors (39) and ( 41) form the complete biorthonormal basis of the Hamiltonian H 0 .
For γ ≪ Ω one finds λ ≈ Ω. For resonant PTsymmetric circuit QED, i.e., as ω r ≈ Ω, the following states form the group of quasi-degenerate levels (the last state is absent for n = 0):
For γ = 0 these states transform in Eqs. (31) and (32).

div.head: B. Effective Hamiltonian for a quasi-degenerate group of eigenstates
div.p: Here, using the main result (21) we obtain the effective Hamiltonian for the group of quasi-degenerate states with n = 0. Then the set P of states that we want to project onto contains only three states: the right eigenvectors are
and the left eigenvectors are
The corresponding energies at g = 0 are

(45) The evaluation of the direct matrix elements of the pertubration ⟨L p | V |R p ′ ⟩ and of the second-order contribution due to indirect transitions in Eq. ( 21) is straightforward but lengthy, so we provide it in Appendix A.

div.head: The explicit expression for the effective Hamiltonian
div.p: Ĥeff is given by
div.formula: ⟨L p | Ĥeff |R p ′ ⟩ =      2i Im λ -g 2 (t * ) 2 ω0+2λ * -4(Im s) 2 ω0 -g 2 |t| 2 Re 1 ω0+2λ -gt -g 2 |t| 2 Re 1 ω0+2λ -2i Im λ -g 2 t 2 ω0+2λ -4(Im s) 2 ω0 -gt * -gt -gt * ω 0 -2 Re λ -4g 2 (Re s) 2 ω0 + Re t 2 ω0+2λ      (46)
div.p: Here, the parameters s and t are the single qubit matrix elements defined in Appendix A 1 a:

The coefficient s is the probability amplitude for the qubit to stay in the same state, while t is the probability amplitude to flip the qubit. Since γ/Ω ≪ 1, the expressions for s and t are simplified as
div.formula: s = ϵ/2 + iγ λ ≈ cos θ + 2iγ Ω sin 2 θ,(49)
t = ∆ 2λ ≈ sin θ - iγ Ω sin 2θ,(50)
div.p: and λ ≈ Ω/2 + iγ cos θ.

To assess the quality of the generic Schrieffer-Wolf transformation and the effective Hamiltonian approximation, we compare in Fig. 5 the real parts of the eigenenergies of the resonant states obtained in two different ways: by direct numerical diagonalization of (22) and diagonalization of the effective Hamiltonian (46). Observe that the energies obtained from the effective Hamiltonian start to slightly deviate around g/Ω ≈ 0.15. However, the third-order EP occurs at small enough g/Ω that is within the region of the validity of the approximation. We shall also point out, that tuning the resonator frequency closer to the qubit's frequency moves the position of the thirdorder EP to smaller values of g/Ω, and, therefore, makes the approximation to work better (see the Sec. VA for details).

The reasons of such a slight disagreement can be understood in the following way. The denominators in Eq. ( 21) contain the energy differences at g = 0. When g increases the energies deviate from their values at g = 0, but as long as these deviations are small in comparison with energy differences

q , we can neglect the renormalization of the energies in Eq. (21). As one can see in Fig. 1, at relatively small values of g, the level repulsion brings the levels from different resonant groups sufficiently close in energy, so that a main assumption of a well-defined groups of quasi-degenerate states becomes invalid. Correspondingly, the effective Hamiltonian approximation can be improved if the energies entering Eq. ( 21) were to be determined in a self-consistent manner.

div.head: C. Parity analysis of the effective Hamiltonian
div.p: In order to provide the parity analysis of PTsymmetric QED, we notice that an exchanging of two qubits corresponds to the parity operator P in the subspace P written as
Using ( 46) and ( 51) one can show that P Ĥeff P-1 = Ĥ † Therefore, the effective Hamiltonian is PT -symmetric and P-pseudo-Hermitian as well.

Using these properties of Ĥeff we apply the parity analysis of Ref. [39] directly to the effective Hamiltonian to qualitatively describe the structure of the emergent EP s. We recall here that the levels of a P-pseudo-Hermitian system can be characterized by their parity indices in the regions of parameters where the corresponding energies stay real. These parity indices are simply the parities of the states in the absence of non-Hermiticity. The formation of second-order EP s is possible then only between the levels with opposite parity indices, and the higherorder EP s can be obtained by tuning several second-order EP s to coalesce.
To facilitate such parity analysis, it is natural to switch to a new basis of right
and left

eigenvectors, in which the parity operator ( 51) is diago-FIG. 5: Comparison of the calculated eigenenergies of the resonant states obtained by direct numerical diagonalization and from the effective Hamiltonian (46). Solid blue lines: real parts of the eigenenergies Re [E/Ω] obtained by direct numerical diagonalization of (22). Dashed red lines: the same, but obtained from the full effective Hamiltonian matrix (46). Dotted black lines: the same, but obtained from the effective Hamiltonian matrix expanded to linear order in γ/Ω. Resonator frequency ratio and the qubit mixing angle are the same as in Figs. 1( nal:
The transformation from the basis ( 43) and ( 44) to the basis ( 52) and ( 53) is described by the matrix F
where
Using (57) we obtain the effective Hamiltonian in the new basis:
div.formula: ⟨L ′ p | Ĥeff |R ′ p ′ ⟩ = F -1 p,p1 ⟨L p1 | Ĥeff |R p ′ 1 ⟩F p ′ 1 ,p ′ = =      g 2 (ℑs) 2 ω0 -Re t 2 +|t| 2 ω0+2λ 2i Im λ + g 2 t 2 2(ω0+2λ) - √ 2g Re t 2i Im λ + g 2 t 2
g 2 4(Im s) 2 ω0 + Re |t| 2 -t 2 ω0+2λ - √ 2ig Im t - √ 2g Re t - √ 2ig Im t ω 0 -2 Re λ -4g 2 (Re s) 2 ω0 + Re t 2 ω0+2λ      (58)
div.p: Taking into account the approximate expressions for s, t and λ, i.e., (49) and ( 50), the effective Hamiltonian can be greatly simplified to
div.formula: ⟨L ′ p | Ĥeff |R ′ p ′ ⟩ ≈    -2g 2 sin 2 θ ωr+Ω 2iγ cos θ - √ 2g sin θ 2iγ cos θ 0 0 - √ 2g sin θ 0 ∆ω -4g 2 cos 2 θ ωr + sin 2 θ ωr+Ω    ,(59)
div.p: where the detuning frequency ∆ω = ω r -Ω is introduced. At γ = 0 and g = 0, the three states ( 43) have the parities 1, -1 and 1 respectively. We identify the first and the third states as the "bright" states, while the second one -as the "dark" state. At γ = 0 , the dark state is decoupled from the bright states and the Hermitian off-diagonal matrix elements, -√ 2g sin θ, couple the two "bright" states driving the avoided crossing between them.

As γ and g are turned on, the middle state (the dark state at γ = 0) with negative parity can go through a second-order EP with either of the other two states, but the first and the third states having the same (positive) parity, never form a second-order EP. This property is reflected in the structure of the matrix (59): the diagonal matrix elements are purely real, and the non-Hermitian off-diagonal matrix elements 2iγ cos θ only couple the middle state with the other two.

The interplay of the avoided crossing between the "bright" states and of the mixing between the "dark" state and one of the "bright" states due to nonhermiticity leads to the precursor behaviour that we observed in Fig. 2(b,c). At g = 0, the two lowest-lying states form a complex conjugated pair. However, when g increases, the avoided crossing leads to the exchange of the eigenvectors of the two "bright" states (first and third) [46]. As a result, the second level forms a complex conjugated pair with the third level instead.

div.head: V. QUASI-DEGENERATE STATES OF PT -SYMMETRIC CIRCUIT QED: PT -SYMMETRY PRESERVED AND BROKEN PHASES
div.p: The eigenvalues spectrum of an arbitrary PTsymmetric quantum system can be of two types, i.e., real or complex conjugate values defining PT -symmetry unbroken (preserved) and broken quantum phases, accordingly. The EP s of different orders separate these phases. By making use of the effective Hamiltonian (59) in the following we identify these phases and the lines of EP s in the group of resonant levels of PT -symmetric circuit QED.
div.formula: A. Phase diagram (γ/Ω -g/Ω) of PT -symmetric circuit QED
div.p: The eigenvalues of the effective Hamiltonian (59) are obtained as the roots of its characteristic polynomial, P (E) = det(E -Ĥ) = 0 [37,[47][48][49]. Since Ĥeff is a 3×3 matrix, the characteristic polynomial, P (E), is written as P (E) = E 3 + bE 2 + cE + d, where the coefficients b, c, d are expressed through the matrix elements of (59) denoted as
div.formula: H p,p ′ ; b = -Tr H p,p ′ ,(60) c
div.p: Because of the PT -symmetry of H p,p ′ , the coefficients of the characteristic polynomial are all purely real [50].
Transforming the characteristic polynomial, P (E), to the dimensionless depressed cubic form, we obtain
where the dimensionless energy Ẽ is
and the coefficients p and q are

Using the standard analysis of a depressed cubic equation (63) (see Appendix B for more details), we find that the equality, p 3 + q 2 = 0, determines the line of secondorder EP s, and a third-order EP is determined by the condition p = q = 0.

The PT -symmetry preserved and broken phases can be quantitatively characterized by the maximal imaginary part of the eigenvalues of Ĥeff and the minimal absolute value of the difference between two eigenvalues as the functions of parameters g/Ω and γ/Ω. It is presented in Fig. 6(a,b).

The Fig. 6(a) allows us to distinguish the region of parameters, where PT -symmetry preserved phase occurs (white area), from the region of parameters where PTsymmetry broken phase occurs (grayed area). The line p 3 + q 2 = 0 (red curve) separates these two phases. The Figure 6(b) allows us to identify degeneracies of the eigenvalues. As we see, the line p 3 + q 2 = 0 borders the region of parameters with purely real eigenvalues and corresponds to the degeneracy of the eigenvalues. As such, p 3 + q 2 = 0 is simply the line of second-order EP s.

The intersection of lines p = 0 and q = 0 (red point) corresponds to the triple-degenerate root of Eq. ( 63) and indicates the third-order EP at g cr /Ω ≃ 0.1375 and γ cr /Ω ≃ 7.65 × 10 -3 . Notice here that the critical value of the interaction strength g cr decreases with ∆ω as g cr ≃ (∆ω)Ω/2. The critical value of the gain/loss parameter γ cr satisfies the similar scaling, however, it acquires an additional degree of smallness due to almost longitudinal coupling θ ≪ 1: γ cr ≃ g cr × θ/ √ 2 (see Appendix C for details).

In principle, triple-degeneracy of the characteristic polynomial root is not the sufficient condition to have a third-order EP. However, we show explicitly in Appendix B, that the triple-degeneracy point is indeed the third-order EP in this case.
B. The eigenvalues under perturbation of parameters γ and g near the third-order EP.

To study the asymptotic behaviour of eigenvalues E 1-3 as the parameters γ and g slightly deviate from the values γ cr and g cr corresponding to the third-order EP (EP 3 ), it is convenient to work directly with the depressed cubic parameters p and q as a curvilinear system of coordinates in the vicinity of the third-order EP. To corroborate this view, we display the lines of constant p and q in Fig. 7.
Considering different directions of perturbation, we classify the behaviour of eigenvalues as follows (the details of the derivation can be found in Appendix D).

Along the line of second-order EP s (case 1), where the condition p 3 + q 2 = 0 is valid, we obtain a single wellseparated eigenvalue, Ẽ1 = -2 3 √ q and doubly-degenerate eigenvalues, Ẽ2,3 = -2 cos 2π 3 × 3 √ q (see Figs. 2c and3c).

The variation of parameters γ and g along the line q = 0, p < 0 (case 2) results in the real spectrum with three distinct eigenvalues, Ẽk = 2 cos π 6 + 2π 3 (k -1) × |p|, where k = 1, 2, 3. Such spectrum is also present in Figs. 2(c) and 3(c). The variation of parameters γ and g along the line q = 0, p > 0 (case 3) results in the spectrum with a single Ẽ1 = 0 and a pair of complex conjugated purely imaginary eigenvalues, Ẽ2-3 = ±2i sin 2π 3 × √ p (see Fig. 2d and3d). Finally, we notice that the variation of parameters γ and g along the line q/p = const (case 4) leads into the PT -symmetry broken phase with the spectrum composed of a single real energy, Ẽ1 = -3 √ 2q and two complex conjugated ones, Ẽ2-3 = -e ±2iπ/3 3 √ 2q. Such spectrum can be also seen in Figs. 2c,d and3c,d.

Depending on the direction of the perturbation in the parameter space, we observe very different behaviour of the eigenvalues that an EP 3 splits into, i.e. anisotropic scaling of eigenvalues that was already reported for second-order EP s [51]. It is worth noting, that in the previous works, the different behaviour of the eignevalues was attributed to different types of EP 3 . For example, the authors of Ref. [48] referred to the behaviour in cases 2 -4 as the EP 3 of types I, III and 0, respectively. In Ref. [47], the behaviour in the case 4 was generically attributed to an EP 3 in a PT -symmetric system, while the behaviour in the case 2 was generically attributed to a symmetric EP 3 in a P-symmetric system. Here we clearly demonstrate that the different behaviour of the eigenvalues under perturbation can be attributed to the very same EP 3 .

div.head: VI. CONCLUSIONS
div.p: To conclude, we extend the formalism of the generalized Schrieffer-Wolff transformation to arbitrary non-Hermitian interacting quantum systems, and derive the effective Hamiltonian taking into account the interaction terms as the perturbation up to the second order. To demonstrate the effectiveness of the method we apply this generic procedure to a basic PT -symmetric circuit QED composed of two non-Hermitian non-interacting superconducting qubits weakly coupled to a lossless resonator of the frequency ω r . In this model the non-Hermiticity is introduced as a staggered imaginary longitudinal magnetic field or gain/loss γ of corresponding qubits states. We consider PT -symmetric circuit QED biased in the resonant regime as the frequency of a single qubit Ω is close to the frequency of the resonator, i.e., Ω ≃ ω r . The resonant regime of a circuit QED is a simplest one providing well-defined groups of quasi-degenerate eigenstates even for moderate values of the coupling strength, g/Ω.

Using the explicit expression for the effective Hamiltonian, we calculate analytically the eigenspectrum of a PT -symmetric circuit QED for different parameters γ and g and identify the PT -symmetry preserved and broken quantum phases, obtain the EP s of different orders. In particular, we obtain the values of parameters γ cr and g cr for the third-order EP s. The values of γ cr and g cr decrease with the detuning ω r -Ω and the mixing angle θ ≪ 1.

We compare our analytical results with the direct numerical diagonalization of the Hamiltonian (22) and find a good agreement in the range of parameters g, γ ≪ 1 for both a longitudinal and transverse coupling between qubits and the resonator. Moreover, for PT quantum circuits with a longitudinal coupling we find a good agreement in a whole range of the interaction strength, g/Ω ≤ 0.4, and as a result one can obtain the position of the third-order EP with a great accuracy. The reason for that is the absence of a substantial mixing between eigenstates of different groups in PT -quantum circuit with a longitudinal coupling.

The most important physical signature of the nonhermiticity is the mixture of "bright" and "dark" states. In particular, in the absence of non-Hermitian terms the energy spectrum of a circuit QED system splits into the subgroups of "bright" and "dark" states having different parity indices, and therefore, these subgroups do not mix with each other [46]. As the non-Hermiticity is turned on, the second-order EP s are formed between the pair of eigenstates with different parity (i.e. the "dark" and a FIG. 6: Calculated maximal imaginary part of the eigenenergy (a) and the minimal absolute value of the distance between two energies (b) as the functions of the interaction strength g/Ω and gain (loss) parameter γ/Ω. The line p 3 + q 2 = 0 separates the regions of PT -symmetry unbroken (preserved) and broken phases (see red curve in the panel (a)). It also corresponds to a degeneracy in the eigenvalues (see panel (b)). The position of the third-order EP is determined as the intersection of p = 0 and q = 0 lines (see panel (a)), and it also coincides with the fold in p 3 + q 2 = 0 curve.

div.head: FIG. 7:
div.p: The lines of constant p and q in the vicinity of the third-order EP. Horizontally aligned green dashed lines: level curves p = 8 × 10 -6 • m for integer m; solid line corresponds to the level p = 0. Vertically aligned purple dash-dot lines: level curves q = 2 × 10 -7 • n for integer n; solid line corresponds to the level q = 0. Orange solid line: level curve p 3 + q 2 = 0.

one of "bright" states) due to P-pseudo-Hermitian symmetry of the Hamiltonian (22). Moreover, the vicinity of the third-order EP is characterized by the interplay of this non-Hermitian mixing and the standard avoided crossing between the "bright" states in the absence of dissipation.

Going beyond the analytical considerations of the present paper, it is important to emphasize that in the vicinity of an n-th order EP, the states involved form an isolated quasi-degenerate group of states. As such, the Schrieffer-Wolff transformation outlined here can be used to numerically derive the local n×n effective Hamil-tonian describing the vicinity of the n th -order EP. Our formalism can also be readily applied to the thermodynamic models in condensed matter where Schrieffer-Wolf transformation is used to obtain the effective low-energy models by integrating out high-energy degrees of freedom.

1. Spin part of the matrix elements Derivation of the spin part of the matrix elements proceeds in two steps. In subsection A 1 a, we compute the single-spin matrix elements ⟨± l |σ z |± r ⟩. In subsection A 1 b, we use this single-spin matrix elements to compute the two-spin matrix elements ⟨± l ±l |(σ z 1 + σz 2 )| ± r ±r ⟩.

div.head: a. Single-spin matrix elements
div.p: Let us start with
div.formula: ⟨+ l |σ z |+ r ⟩ = 1 ∆ 2 2 + ϵ 2 + iγ + λ 2 ϵ 2 + iγ + λ, ∆ 2 ϵ 2 + iγ + λ -∆ 2 = = 1 2λ ϵ 2 + iγ + λ ϵ 2 + iγ + λ ∆ 2 ϵ 2 + iγ + λ -∆ 2 = = ϵ 2 + iγ + λ -∆ 2 2 2λ ϵ 2 + iγ + λ = = 2 ϵ 2 + iγ ϵ 2 + iγ + λ 2λ ϵ 2 + iγ + λ = ϵ 2 + iγ λ = s,(A3)
div.p: Here, we used the following two identities to factorize first the denominator and then the nominator:
After that, we proceed with the other matrix elements in a straightforward manner.
b. Action of σz 1 + σz 2 on two-spin states and the two-spin matrix elements.

Here, we use the single-spin matrix elements obtained in the previous section. Instead of directly computing the two-spin matrix elements, it is more convenient to compute the action of (σ z 1 + σz 2 ) on the two-spin states. The coefficients of the expansion then give the desired matrix elements. Notice that the matrix elements are symmetric with respect to the exchange of the initial and the final states. We first start we the second term in Eq. ( 21) for the effective Hamiltonian. Here, we consider the last term in Eq. ( 21). We denote ∆ Ĥ(2) = -1 2
div.formula: q∈Q 1 E 0 q -E 0 p + 1 E 0 q -E 0 p ′ × × |R p ⟩⟨L p | V |R q ⟩⟨L q | V |R p ′ ⟩⟨L p ′ |, (A16) ⟨L p |∆ Ĥ(2) |R p ′ ⟩ = - 1 2 q∈Q 1 E 0 q -E 0 p + 1 E 0 q -E 0 p ′ × × ⟨L p | V |R q ⟩⟨L q | V |R p ′ ⟩ (A17)
div.p: To compute the matrix elements of ∆ Ĥ(2) , we need to find the possible intermediate q-states for the given p, p ′ matrix element. Below we list the intermediate states in a table. Substituting the results of this section and the expressions for the energies at g = 0 (45) into Eq. ( 21), we finally obtain the effective Hamiltonian in its matrix form:

figure.head: FIG. 1 :
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1: Calculated lowest-lying energy levels of the Hermitian circuit QED (γ = 0) biased in the resonant regime. The mixing angle was chosen as: a) θ = π/40 = 4.5 • providing an almost longitudinal coupling; b) θ = π/2 -π/40 = 85.5 • providing an almost transverse coupling. Solid (dashed) curve denotes the even (odd) parity of the levels, respectively. The dashed rectangles show the parts of the spectrum that are zoomed in Figs. 2 (middle one of panel (a)), 3 (upper one of panel (a)), and 4 (the single one of panel (b)).

figure.head: FIG. 2 :
figure.figDesc: FIG.2: Calculated real parts Re [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies of the quasi-degenerate states as functions of the normalized interaction strength g/Ω for different values of γ/Ω. The panels zoom on the three middle levels of Fig.1(a). The widths of the shaded ribbons are proportional to the imaginary parts Im [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies. Solid (dashed) lines correspond to positive (negative) parity of the states at γ = 0. The qubit mixing angle θ was taken the same as for Fig.1(a).

figure.head: FIG. 3 :
figure.figDesc: FIG.3: Calculated real parts Re [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies of the quasi-degenerate states as functions of the normalized interaction strength g/Ω for various values of γ/Ω. The panels zoom on the four upper levels of Fig.1a. The widths of the shaded ribbons are proportional to the imaginary parts Im [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies. Solid (dashed) lines correspond to positive (negative) parity of the states at γ = 0. The qubit mixing angle is the same as for Fig.1(a).

figure.head: FIG. 4 :
figure.figDesc: FIG.4: Calculated real parts Re [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies of the lowest-lying levels as functions of the normalized interaction strength g/Ω for various values of γ/Ω. The widths of the shaded ribbons are proportional to the imaginary parts Im [E/Ω] of the eigenenergies. The resonator frequency is chosen ωr/Ω = 1.07. The qubit mixing angle θ = π/2 -π/40. Solid (dashed) lines correspond to positive (negative) parity of the states at γ = 0.

FIG.5: Comparison of the calculated eigenenergies of the resonant states obtained by direct numerical diagonalization and from the effective Hamiltonian(46). Solid blue lines: real parts of the eigenenergies Re [E/Ω] obtained by direct numerical diagonalization of(22). Dashed red lines: the same, but obtained from the full effective Hamiltonian matrix(46). Dotted black lines: the same, but obtained from the effective Hamiltonian matrix expanded to linear order in γ/Ω. Resonator frequency ratio and the qubit mixing angle are the same as in Figs. 1(a), 2 and 3: ωr/Ω = 1.07 and θ = π/40.

figure.head: (σ z 1 + σz 2 )
figure.figDesc: | + r -r ⟩ = (s|+ r ⟩ -t|r ⟩) ⊗ | -r ⟩+ + |+ r ⟩ ⊗ (-s * | -r ⟩ -t * | +r ⟩) = = (s -s * )| + r -r ⟩ -t|r -r ⟩ -t * | + r +r ⟩, (A9) (σ z 1 + σz 2 )|r +r ⟩ = -(s|r ⟩ + t|+ r ⟩) ⊗ | +r ⟩+ + |r ⟩ ⊗ (s * | +r ⟩ -t * | -r ⟩) = = -(s -s * )|r +r ⟩ -t| + r +r ⟩ -t * |r -r ⟩, (A10) (σ z 1 + σz 2 )|r -r ⟩ = -(s|r ⟩ -t|+ r ⟩) ⊗ | -r ⟩--|r ⟩ ⊗ (s * | -r ⟩ -t * | +r ⟩) = = -(s + s * )|r -r ⟩ -t| + r -r ⟩ -t * |r +r ⟩, (A11) (σ z 1 + σz 2 )| + r +r ⟩ = (s|+ r ⟩ -t|r ⟩) ⊗ | +r ⟩+ + |+ r ⟩ ⊗ (s * | +r ⟩ -t * | -r ⟩) = = (s + s * )| + r +r ⟩ -t|r +r ⟩ -t * | + r -r ⟩. (A12)As we have mentioned, the coefficients of the expansions in the right-hand sides gives us the matrix elements that we want. For example, ⟨+ l -l |(σ z 1 + σz 2 )| + r -r ⟩ = (s -s * ). (A13)
Matrix elements of the effective Hamiltonian for n = 0 . a. The direct matrix elements of the perturbation.

⟨+ l -l 0| V |r -r 1⟩ = ⟨l -l 1| V | + r -r 0⟩ = = ⟨l -l |(σ z 1 + σz 2 )| + r -r ⟩⟨1|a † |0⟩ = -t (A14)Analogously,⟨l +l 0| V |r -r 1⟩ = ⟨l -l 1| V |r +r 0⟩ = -t * . (A15)b. The quadratic corrections arising from non-direct matrix elements.

TABLE I: Allowed intermediate states for the indirect transitions. The first column lists the final states, while the first row lists the initial states. -0, --2, + -2, -+2The matrix elements read⟨+ l -l 0|∆ Ĥ(2) | + r -r 0⟩ = -(s -s * ) 2 ω 0 -(t * ) 2 ω 0 + 2λ * = = Im s) 2 ω 0 -(t * ) 2 ω 0 + 2λ * , (A18) ⟨l +l 0|∆ Ĥ(2) |r +r 0⟩ = -(s -s * ) ⟨+ l -l 0|∆ Ĥ(2) |r +r 0⟩ = ⟨l +l 0|∆ Ĥ(2) | + r -r 0⟩ = took into account that |⟨1|a † |0⟩| 2 = 1 and |⟨2|a † |1⟩| 2 = 2.c. Final form.
t * ) 2 ω0+2λ * -4(Im s) 2Since at θ ≪ 1, u 1 ≪ u 2 , the right-hand-side of Eq. (C7) is very narrowly peaked at g2 = ∆/u 2 . As such, we can approximate with a good accuracy

row.cell: Substituting it into Eq. (C6), we find then
Finally, we can go back to dimensional quantities to
t 2 ω0+2λ -4(Im s) 2 ω0 -gt t 2 -gt * ω0+2λ

note.p: * ω 0 -2 Re λ -4g 2 (Re s) 2 ω0 + Re

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: We acknowledge the financial support of Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (Projekt EF 11/10-2) and the financial support through the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation program under grant agreement No 863313 'Supergalax'.
Appendix A: Derivation of the matrix elements of the effective Hamiltonian.
To derive the matrix elements of the effective Hamiltonian, we first need to compute the matrix elements of the perturbation:
The photon part of the matrix elements of V is obtained as:

The spin part of the matrix elements is computed in A 1. After that in A 2, we compute the direct and indirect contributions to the effective Hamiltonian obtaining Eq. (46). The three solutions of Eq. ( 63) are written explicitly as [52] Ẽ1 = α + β, Ẽ2 = e 2iπ/3 α + e -2iπ/3 β, (B1) Ẽ3 = e -2iπ/3 α + e 2iπ/3 β, where α =

In the last expression, it is implied that the root branches are chosen in such a way, that the equalities are satisfied. Finally, the eigenenergies of the effective Hamiltonian matrix can be obtained by transformation (64). The combination (p 3 +q 2 ), appearing inside the square roots in Eqs. (B2) and (B3) is proportional to the discriminant of the depressed cubic (63) and its sign can be used to characterize the roots of (63) and, as a consequence, the eigenvalues of the effective Hamiltonian matrix [50,52]:
> 0, there is one real root and a pair of complex conjugated roots;
• if (p 3 + q 2 ) < 0, there are three real roots;
• if (p 3 + q 2 ) = 0, there is a double root, which corresponds to a second-order EP.

The Equation (63) has a single triple-degenerate root if both coefficients of the depressed cubic are zero: p = q = 0. Graphically, we can identify the location of the triple-degeneracy point by looking for the intersection of p = 0 and q = 0 lines (see Fig. 6(a)). Equivalently, the same point corresponds to the touching of p 3 + q 2 = 0 and p = 0 lines or to the fold in p 3 + q 2 = 0 line.

The triple-degeneracy of the eigenvalues is a necessary condition to have a third-order EP, but, in principle, it is not sufficient one: the eigenvectors also need to be tripledegenerate. Let g ′ /Ω and γ ′ /Ω be the parameters corresponding to the triple-degenerate eigenvalue E ′ of the effective Hamiltonian matrix (46). The point (g ′ Ω, γ ′ /Ω) will correspond to a third-order EP, if the equation on eigenvectors

has only a single solution. As we know from the theory of systems of linear equations, it happens if and only if the matrix (H p,p ′ -E ′ δ p,p ′ ) has rank 2, which is equivalent to the requirement that there is at least one non-zero second-order minor of the matrix [52].
Using the approximated form of the matrix (59) that works very well in the vicinity of the third-order EP, we can compute the minor
This directly confirms that the triple-degeneracy point corresponding to the intersection of p = 0 and q = 0 lines in Fig. ( 6)(a) is truly a third-order EP.
Appendix C: Scaling of the critical values of the interaction strength and the gain/loss parameter.

In the following, it is convenient to introduce the dimensionless parameters of the interaction strength g = g/Ω and gain/loss γ = γ/Ω as well as dimensionless detuning ∆ = (ω r -Ω)/Ω. With the help of these definitions, effective Hamiltonian (59) reads
To make further derivations tractable, we also denote
The critical values of g and γ correspond to the solution of the system of equations p, q = 0. We can substitute Eq. (61) into Eq. ( 62) to find
As such, we can conveniently rewrite the system of equations p, q = 0 as
Directly substituting Eq. (C1) into Eqs. (60), ( 61) and (62) and then the obtained results into Eqs. (C4) and (C5), we arrive at the system
Appendix D: Perturbation of eigenvalues away from the third-order EP.
1. Perturbation along the line p 3 + q 2 = 0.
The discriminant equation p 3 + q 2 = 0 defines the corresponding curve implicitly. We can turn it into explicit equation
The characteristic fold at p = q = 0 can then be attributed to the singular behaviour of the first derivative -2/(3 3 √ q) at q = 0.
When we move away from the point p = q = 0 along the line (D1), we observe one singly-degenerate real root Ẽ1 = -2 3 √ q, (D2)
and one doubly-degenerate real root Ẽ2,3 = -2 cos 2π 3 × 3 √ q (D3)
2. Perturbation along the line q = 0, p < 0.
In this case, the parameter α is
And the parameter β is
As such, we observe three distinct real roots
3. Perturbation along the line q = 0, p > 0.
In this case,
We observe one real root E 1 = 0 and a pair of complex conjugated purely imaginary roots
4. Perturbation along the line q/p = const.
For |q|, |p| ≪ 1, we can neglect p 3 in comparison with q 2 . As such, we can write approximately α, β = 3 -q ± |q|.
There are three distinct roots then. One real: Ẽ1 = -3 2q, (D11) and a pair of complex conjugated ones E 2 = -e 2iπ/3 3 2q, E 3 = -e -2iπ/3 3 2q. (D12)

Microwave photonmediated interactions between semiconductor qubits

Experimental determination of PTsymmetric exceptional points in a single trapped ion
Non-hermitian dynamics and PTsymmetry breaking in interacting mesoscopic rydberg platforms
Observation of parity-time symmetry breaking transitions in a dissipative floquet system of ultracold atoms
Model of a PT -symmetric bose-einstein condensate in a δ-function double-well potential
